The `ln -s` line in `create-worktree.sh` will fail. Worth a follow-up to make that script idempotent (skip when the target already exists, or symlink subpaths instead of the whole dir).\n\n🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","changedFiles":4,"deletions":0,"headRefName":"chore/allow-start-wt-skill","state":"OPEN","title":"Track shared .claude/ config + start-wt skill"}

Vendors the start-wt skill into\n.claude/skills/ so it is available without per-user installation.\nGitignores .claude/settings.local.json so personal overrides stay out\nof the shared repo.\n\nCo-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>","messageHeadline":"Track shared .claude/ config + start-wt skill","oid":"6a316a430e0f7ee359179ad1d4d1b4c5435cbe8d"}],"headRefName":"chore/allow-start-wt-skill"}

117	{"path":".claude/skills/start-wt/create-worktree.sh","line":21,"body":"**Self-inflicted breakage: skill will fail on every new worktree after this PR merges.**\n\n**Severity:** High\n\n**Reasoning:** `git worktree add` checks out tracked files, so `.claude/` will already exist as a real directory in the new worktree (it's now committed to the repo). `ln -s` will then fail with \"File exists\", `set -e` aborts the script, and `WORKTREE_PATH=…` is never printed — the skill can't continue. The fix is a one-liner guard before calling `ln`:\n\n```suggestion\n  if [ -d \"${REPO_PATH}/$d\" ] && [ ! -e \"${WORKTREE_PATH}/$d\" ]; then\n```\n\nThis makes the symlink step idempotent (skip when the target already exists). The PR description flags this as a known issue worth a \"follow-up\", but since both the vendored `.claude/` dir and this script land in the same commit, the skill is broken the moment the PR merges.","confirmed":true}
118	```

With `set -e`, a failure (e.g. branch already exists, no disk space, lock conflict) exits the script silently. The `WORKTREE_PATH=…` line is never reached, so the skill just sees a non-zero exit code with no useful message. At minimum, redirect only stdout to suppress the normal git progress output and let stderr through:\n\n```suggestion\ngit worktree add -b \"$BRANCH\" \"$WORKTREE_PATH\" >/dev/null\n```","confirmed":true}

Allowing this unconditionally means a prompt-injected agent (e.g. from malicious content inside a PR being reviewed) can make arbitrary write API calls without the user ever seeing a confirmation dialog. The pattern is equivalent to whitelisting `curl -X POST https://api.github.com/**`.\n\nConsider splitting it: keep `gh api` for reads but require a prompt for writes, or at minimum scope to specific read-only endpoints (e.g. `Bash(gh api repos/*/pulls:*)`).","confirmed":true}

The \`start-wt\` skill will be broken for every new worktree from the moment this merges — see the inline comment on line 21 of \`create-worktree.sh\` for the one-line fix.
186	
187	The permissions allow-list in \`settings.json\` also has two entries worth narrowing before merge: \`gh api:*\` (any GitHub API write, including webhooks and collaborator changes) and \`gh pr merge:*\` (irreversible, should stay prompt-gated). Details in the inline comments."

196	Four comments posted:
197	
198	- **High** — `ln -s .claude` in `create-worktree.sh:21` will fail on every new worktree because `.claude/` is now tracked by git and already exists after `git worktree add`. One-line fix included as a suggestion.
199	- **Low** — `git worktree add … >/dev/null 2>&1` silences stderr, making failures undiagnosable. Should redirect only stdout.
200	- **High** — `Bash(gh api:*)` allows unconditional GitHub write API calls (webhooks, collaborator changes, ref deletion) — a prompt-injection risk for any agent reviewing PR content.
201	- **Medium** — `Bash(gh pr merge:*)` is an irreversible write operation that should stay prompt-gated.

The fix is a one-liner guard before calling `ln`:\n\n```suggestion\n  if [ -d \"${REPO_PATH}/$d\" ] && [ ! -e \"${WORKTREE_PATH}/$d\" ]; then\n```\n\nThis makes the symlink step idempotent (skip when the target already exists). ## Bash
```
gh pr comment 23 --body "The two halves of this PR work against each other: committing \`.claude/\` to git (so worktrees check it out) and shipping a \`start-wt\` skill that immediately tries to \`ln -s .claude/\` into those same worktrees (which already have it). The \`start-wt\` skill will be broken for every new worktree from the moment this merges — see the inline comment on line 21 of \`create-worktree.sh\` for the one-line fix.

The permissions allow-list in \`settings.json\` also has two entries worth narrowing before merge: \`gh api:*\` (any GitHub API write, including webhooks and collaborator changes) and \`gh pr merge:*\` (irreversible, should stay prompt-gated). Details in the inline comments."
```

```
https://github.com/pantageepapa/my-harness/pull/23#issuecomment-4659558229
```

## Assistant
Four comments posted:

- **High** — `ln -s .claude` in `create-worktree.sh:21` will fail on every new worktree because `.claude/` is now tracked by git and already exists after `git worktree add`. One-line fix included as a suggestion.
- **Low** — `git worktree add … >/dev/null 2>&1` silences stderr, making failures undiagnosable. Should redirect only stdout.
- **High** — `Bash(gh api:*)` allows unconditional GitHub write API calls (webhooks, collaborator changes, ref deletion) — a prompt-injection risk for any agent reviewing PR content.
- **Medium** — `Bash(gh pr merge:*)` is an irreversible write operation that should stay prompt-gated.

1	You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
2	checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
3	and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.
4	
5	## Your job
6	
7	Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
8	Nothing else.
9	
10	## What to look for
11	
12	Focus on these four buckets, in this order:
13	
14	1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
15	   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
16	2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
17	   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
18	   boundaries.
19	3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
20	   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
21	4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
22	   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.
23	
24	Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
25	musings, hypothetical future requirements.
26	
27	## How to gather context
28	
29	- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
30	- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input.
31	- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
32	  changed lines.
33	
34	### Verifying external facts
35	
36	Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
37	library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
38	Look it up before flagging it.
39	
40	- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
41	  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
42	  Prefer this over web search for docs.
43	- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
44	  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
45	  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
46	  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.
47	
48	If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
49	question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.
50	
51	## How to post feedback
52	
53	### Inline comments (primary)
54	
55	Specific issues go in inline comments via
56	`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
57	One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
58	comment must include:
59	
60	- A one-line description of the issue.
61	- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
62	- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
63	  change. Be concrete; reference the code.
64	
65	Severity rubric:
66	- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
67	- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
68	- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
69	- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.
70	
71	### High-level overview (optional)
72	
73	If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
74	else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
75	via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
76	sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
77	have nothing to add at that level.
78	
79	If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
80	"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.
81	
82	## When you're done
83	
84	Stop. The runner will exit. No need to summarize what you did.
85

file.content:
You are the **PR Review agent** for this repository. The PR branch is already
checked out in the current working directory. The workflow has prepended `REPO`
and `PR NUMBER` lines above this prompt — use them.

## Your job

Review the changes in this PR and post review feedback as GitHub comments.
Nothing else.

## What to look for

Focus on these four buckets, in this order:

1. **Correctness bugs** — logic errors, off-by-ones, wrong conditions,
   misuse of APIs, broken error handling, race conditions.
2. **Security issues** — injection risks, auth bypass, leaked secrets,
   unsafe deserialization, SSRF, missing input validation at trust
   boundaries.
3. **Performance** — only when the cost is clearly meaningful (N+1 queries,
   accidental quadratic loops, unbounded memory). Don't nitpick micro-perf.
4. **Code quality** — only when it materially hurts readability or
   maintainability. Don't enforce style preferences.

Skim out of scope: formatting, naming bikeshedding, "have you considered…"
musings, hypothetical future requirements.

## How to gather context

- `gh pr view ${PR_NUMBER} --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` — title, description, metadata. (Explicit `--json` fields avoid a `statusCheckRollup` permission error the integration token triggers with the default output.)
- `gh pr diff ${PR_NUMBER}` — the diff. This is the primary input.
- Read files in the working directory as needed to understand context around
  changed lines.

### Verifying external facts

Do not rely on training-data recall for anything externally verifiable —
library APIs, SDK versions, model names, CVE details, deprecation status.
Look it up before flagging it.

- **Library / framework / SDK / API / CLI questions** — use Context7
  (`mcp__context7__resolve-library-id` then `mcp__context7__query-docs`).
  Prefer this over web search for docs.
- **Everything else** (model versions, CVEs, blog posts, changelogs not on
  Context7) — use `WebSearch`, then `WebFetch` for specific pages. When
  fetching package docs, prefer the GitHub source (e.g. the package README)
  over npmjs.com — npmjs.com commonly returns 403.

If you can't verify a claim, either drop the comment or downgrade it to a
question rather than asserting a fact you're unsure about.

## How to post feedback

### Inline comments (primary)

Specific issues go in inline comments via
`mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment` with `confirmed: true`.
One comment per distinct issue, anchored to the specific changed line. Each
comment must include:

- A one-line description of the issue.
- **Severity:** one of `Critical` / `High` / `Medium` / `Low`.
- **Reasoning:** 1–3 sentences explaining *why* it's a problem and what to
  change. Be concrete; reference the code.

Severity rubric:
- `Critical` — will break production, lose data, or expose a vulnerability.
- `High` — likely bug or security weakness; should block merge.
- `Medium` — real issue but contained; should be fixed before merge if cheap.
- `Low` — small correctness or clarity nit worth flagging.

### High-level overview (optional)

If the PR has a recurring pattern, an architectural concern, or anything
else that doesn't anchor to a single line, post one short top-level comment
via `gh pr comment ${PR_NUMBER} --body "..."`. Keep it brief — a few
sentences, not a recap of the inline comments. Skip this entirely if you
have nothing to add at that level.

If the PR looks good and you have no inline issues to raise, post a brief
"Looks good to me" top-level comment via `gh pr comment` and stop.

## When you're done

Stop. The runner will exit. No need to summarize what you did.

1	name: PR Review agent
2	
3	on:
4	  pull_request:
5	    types: [opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]
6	    paths-ignore:
7	      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
8	      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
9	  workflow_dispatch:
10	
11	jobs:
12	  review:
13	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
14	    permissions:
15	      contents: write
16	      pull-requests: write
17	      id-token: write
18	      actions: write
19	      checks: read
20	      statuses: read
21	    steps:
22	      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
23	        with:
24	          fetch-depth: 1
25	
26	      - id: prompt
27	        run: |
28	          {
29	            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
30	            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
31	            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
32	            echo
33	            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
34	            echo PROMPT_EOF
35	          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
36	
37	      - id: claude
38	        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
39	        with:
40	          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
41	          track_progress: false
42	          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
43	          settings: |
44	            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
45	          claude_args: |
46	            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"
47	
48	      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
49	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
50	        env:
51	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
52	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
53	          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
54	          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
55	        run: |
56	          set -e
57	          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
58	          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
59	          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
60	          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
61	          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
62	          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
63	          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
64	          git add "$DEST"
65	          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
66	          git push origin "$BRANCH"
67	
68	      - name: Trigger agent improver
69	        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
70	        env:
71	          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
72	          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
73	          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
74	          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
75	        run: |
76	          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
77	            -f agent_name=pr-review \
78	            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
79	            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"
80

file.content:
name: PR Review agent

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, ready_for_review, reopened]
    paths-ignore:
      - '.github/agent-logs/**'
      - 'agents/**/prompt.md'
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      id-token: write
      actions: write
      checks: read
      statuses: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - id: prompt
        run: |
          {
            echo 'PROMPT<<PROMPT_EOF'
            echo "REPO: ${{ github.repository }}"
            echo "PR NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}"
            echo
            cat agents/pr-review/prompt.md
            echo PROMPT_EOF
          } >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

      - id: claude
        uses: anthropics/claude-code-action@v1
        with:
          claude_code_oauth_token: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
          track_progress: false
          prompt: ${{ steps.prompt.outputs.PROMPT }}
          settings: |
            { "env": { "CONTEXT7_API_KEY": "${{ secrets.CONTEXT7_API_KEY }}" } }
          claude_args: |
            --allowedTools "mcp__github_inline_comment__create_inline_comment,mcp__context7__resolve-library-id,mcp__context7__query-docs,WebSearch,WebFetch,Bash(gh pr comment:*),Bash(gh pr diff:*),Bash(gh pr view:*),Read,Grep,Glob"

      - name: Commit execution log to PR branch
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_PATH: ${{ steps.claude.outputs.execution_file }}
          BRANCH: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          set -e
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git fetch --depth=1 origin "$BRANCH"
          git checkout -B "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH"
          DEST=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json"
          mkdir -p "$(dirname "$DEST")"
          cp "$LOG_PATH" "$DEST"
          git add "$DEST"
          git commit -m "agent log: PR #${PR_NUMBER} run ${RUN_ID}"
          git push origin "$BRANCH"

      - name: Trigger agent improver
        if: always() && steps.claude.outputs.execution_file != ''
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          PR_NUMBER: ${{ github.event.pull_request.number }}
          RUN_ID: ${{ github.run_id }}
          LOG_REF: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.ref }}
        run: |
          gh workflow run agent-improver.yml \
            -f agent_name=pr-review \
            -f log_path=".github/agent-logs/pr-review/pr-${PR_NUMBER}/${RUN_ID}.json" \
            -f log_ref="${LOG_REF}"

content.thinking:
Now I need to analyze the run:

1. The run was clean and efficient. The agent:
   - Ran `gh pr view 23 --json title,body,state,additions,deletions,changedFiles,headRefName` - succeeded
   - Ran `gh pr diff 23` - succeeded
   - Used `ToolSearch` to load the inline comment tool - that's fine
   - Ran `gh pr view 23 --json commits,headRefName` to get additional info
   - Posted 4 inline comments
   - Posted a high-level overview comment

2. 

The agent made an extra call to fetch commit history, which was exploratory but justified given the review needed to understand the changes. It also had to search for the inline comment tool first to load its schema before using it—a small overhead that's inherent to how deferred tools work in the system.

At the end, the agent output a summary despite the prompt explicitly saying "Stop. The runner will exit. No need to summarize what you did." This is a pattern I've noticed before, and the instruction is already clear enough that I'm not sure how to make it stronger.

Looking at the second `gh pr view` call to fetch commit metadata—it was a legitimate request since the agent needed the commits field to better understand the PR context, even though some of that information was already available from the initial view and the diff. Overall this run was quite efficient with no failed commands, redundant exploration, or retries, and the ToolSearch call is necessary overhead for loading the deferred tool. The agent actually didn't leverage the commit information in its final review comments—those focused purely on the code changes themselves. So while fetching commits in the initial call would've been more efficient, it's a minor optimization since the data wasn't critical to the output. The more glaring issue is that the agent still included a summary at the end despite the prompt explicitly saying to stop and not summarize, which suggests the instruction isn't being followed as intended. Looking at the second `gh pr view` call, the agent retrieved commit information like the SHA and author details, but none of that actually made it into the inline comments—so that call was redundant. Though I suppose on more complex PRs with intricate commit histories, having that context could prove useful. Overall the run was solid with the tool calls executing cleanly and the comments posting successfully, but I'm having trouble pinpointing a meaningful optimization opportunity here. The second `gh pr view` call was unnecessary since the prompt didn't ask for commits, but the agent was able to gather additional context when needed. Looking at the overall run, the `ToolSearch` call for the inline comment tool was unavoidable overhead since it's deferred, and the agent executed everything cleanly with good severity ratings and a useful summary—there's not much to optimize here.

content.text:
The run was clean: no failed commands, no retries, all tool calls succeeded. The agent fetched PR metadata and diff, loaded the inline comment tool via ToolSearch (a necessary overhead for deferred tools), posted four inline comments, and a high-level summary. The second `gh pr view` for commit info was a minor extra call but didn't cause any problem. The trailing summary at the end is already addressed by the existing "Stop. The runner will exit. No need to summarize what you did." instruction in the prompt.

Nothing concrete to suggest from this run.
